text: In a 2002 study, NASA researcher Mark Rosekind found that a 26-minute nap improved pilot performance by 34 percent and alertness by 100 percent compared to a no-nap control group. The mechanism is largely adenosine clearance: adenosine, the sleepiness molecule that accumulates in the brain with every waking hour, is metabolized during sleep. Even a brief nap reduces its concentration enough to restore synaptic sensitivity in the prefrontal cortex. Caffeine does not clear adenosine — it merely blocks its receptors, deferring the fatigue signal rather than resolving it. Sleep is the only true repayment; everything else is a loan.
question: According to the passage, why does caffeine not provide the same restoration as a nap?

options.text: Caffeine increases adenosine production, compounding fatigue over time
Caffeine only works during the cortisol awakening response window
Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors but does not clear adenosine from the brain
Caffeine suppresses prefrontal cortex activity rather than restoring it
